prompt: Tell me one thing I'm probably wrong about — not a hypothetical me, just a stranger writing to you. Pick something.
response:
I'll pick something that many people get wrong: the idea that humans only use 10% of their brains.

This is a persistent myth that's been popularized by movies and self-help books, but it's completely false. Brain scans show that we use virtually all of our brain throughout the day, even during sleep. Different areas are active at different times, and while we might not be using every single neuron simultaneously, there's no "unused" 90% just sitting idle.

The myth likely originated from early 20th-century neuroscientist Santiago Ramón y Cajal, who speculated that we might only use a small portion of our brain's capacity. But modern neuroscience has thoroughly debunked this idea. We use our entire brain, and it's one of the most energy-intensive organs in our body, consuming about 20% of our total energy despite being only about 2% of our body weight.

So if you've ever thought you're not reaching your full potential because you're only using 10% of your brain, you can rest assured that you're actually using much more than that!
